amber.description: Catalysts, regulatory proteins, molecular machines, receptors

black.description: Terminal products, cellular outcomes, equilibrium states, system endpoints

darkSkyBlue.description: Biochemical reactions, transport, modifications, transcription, translation

green.description: External signals, nutrient availability, environmental conditions, initial states

lightCyan.description: Metabolites, molecular complexes, cellular states, compounds

purple.description: Multi-component requirements, signal integration, convergence points

yellow.description: Decision points with alternative pathways (yes/no branching)

description: Sulfur metabolism in E. coli assimilates inorganic sulfate or organic sulfur sources for cysteine and methionine biosynthesis. Sulfate is taken up by CysPUWA ABC transporter, activated to APS by CysD, reduced to sulfite by CysH, then to sulfide by CysIJ. Sulfide combines with O-acetylserine (from serine) via CysK/CysM to form cysteine. Alternative pathways use thiosulfate, sulfite, or organic sulfur. Regulation is via CysB activator responding to N-acetylserine.

name: Sulfur Assimilation and Cysteine Biosynthesis
notes: Sulfur metabolism shows high OR gates (8) for alternative sulfur sources and branch points.
scientificAccuracy: Based on comprehensive characterization of E. coli cys genes. All enzymes validated.

sources.title: Regulation of cysteine biosynthesis in Escherichia coli and Salmonella typhimurium.
The cysK and cysM gene products of Escherichia coli catalyze the in vivo formation of cysteine from thiosulfate and sulfide, respectively.
sources.authors: Tai GC, Kredich NM, Tai PC, Wu JY.

sources.title: The transcriptional activator CysB and the global regulator IscR control the expression of the sulfate assimilation genes in Escherichia coli.
sources.authors: Koga H, Nakano S, Fujie A, Tomatsu H, Ishihama A, Oshima T.
